text: This is a case report of a robotic-assisted common biotic exploration for choledocholithiasis. This procedure was performed at Sinai Grace Hospital, part of the Detroit Medical Center. This is a video of a fifty-three-year-old male who presented to the hospital with right upper quadrant pain. He had an ultrasound that showed choledocholithiasis and the gallbladder appeared normal. His total bilirubin was three point five and direct bilirubin was three point zero. We typically do not perform ERCPs on patients with choledocholithiasis unless they have ascending cholangitis. This patient's white blood cell count was completely normal and he had no evidence of cholangitis.

The surgeon carefully dissects the omentum from the gallbladder, avoiding injury to the transverse colon. The duodenum, adherent to the gallbladder, is meticulously separated. Fibrofatty tissue is removed from the hepatocystic triangle, and Firefly mode is used to visualize the common bile duct. A clip is placed below the gallbladder infundibulum after milking the cystic duct. The cystic duct is incised for cholangiogram insertion, using a 5 French cholangiocatheter with a wire for stiffness. Arm one of the robot is undocked to allow the C-arm access from the right side, while arm four holds the cholangiocatheter in place for contrast injection during the cholangiogram.

The surgeon observes filling defects in the common bile duct with contrast still flowing into the duodenum. A larger catheter is used, and a balloon is inserted for duct dilation. Initially, the balloon encounters difficulty due to an obstructing stone. The balloon is inflated for three minutes, achieving eight millimeters of dilation, allowing the choledochoscope to pass. The choledochoscope reveals the right and left hepatic branches, the bifurcation, and a large stone in the common bile duct, with visible blood from the insertion.

The surgeon performs a choledochotomy for direct exploration of the common bile duct due to a large stone. A 4-French Fogarty balloon is passed multiple times to dislodge and float the stones for retrieval. The fat is cleared from the anterior aspect of the common bile duct, and the falciform ligament is lifted for retraction. The cystic duct is clipped, and the gallbladder is removed to improve visualization. An anterior choledochotomy is made between the three and nine o'clock positions, followed by irrigation to aid in stone retrieval. Several stones are immediately retrieved, with continued irrigation allowing for the removal of a total of seven stones from the common bile duct.

The surgeon uses the choledochoscope to inspect the common bile duct for any remaining stones or debris. An irrigating catheter is passed both proximally and distally to clear any small debris. Glucagon is administered to dilate the sphincter of Oddi, and once the duct is confirmed clear, the choledochotomy is closed.

The surgeon uses a 3-0 monofilament absorbable suture in an interrupted fashion to close the choledochotomy, ensuring the common bile duct remains open due to its dilation to 1.5 centimeters. The closure is performed under ten times magnification. After closing the choledochotomy, clips are removed from the cystic duct to perform a completion cholangiogram, which shows resolution of filling defects and contrast flowing to the bifurcation of the hepatic ducts and into the duodenum. Clips are reapplied to the cystic duct, completing the procedure.

A fifteen French drain was then left. The patient was started on a diet immediately in the postoperative period. The drain was removed on postoperative day two and bilirubin levels normalized on postoperative day two prior to discharge. While the utility of a robotic-assisted CBD exploration was demonstrated at SAGEs in as shown here, our case highlights that this technique can be used as an alternative to ERCP for choledocholithiasis.
